data.title: The Tournament at Rouen
data.premise: Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein has arrived at the prestigious Tournament of Rouen, where victory could cement his reputation among the nobility. But whispers spread through the pavilions—some question his true identity, and a mysterious herald carries sealed letters that could expose everything.
data.central_question: Can William maintain his noble facade and win the tournament while dangerous secrets threaten to destroy his carefully constructed identity?
data.healing_context: Wounds heal naturally over days and weeks with rest and proper care. Tournament injuries are common—bruises and cuts mend in days, broken bones require weeks. Skilled healers can set bones and stitch wounds, but serious injuries could end a knight's career permanently.
starting_inventory: a suit of well-maintained plate armor
forged papers of nobility from Gelderland
a leather purse with 25 gold florins
a knight's sword with worn grip
a heraldic shield bearing the arms of Liechtenstein

setting.name: The Tournament Grounds at Rouen
setting.description: A sprawling field outside the Norman city, filled with colorful pavilions, practice yards, and wooden stands for spectators. Knights from across Europe have gathered to test their mettle in the lists, while merchants, nobles, and commoners mingle in a festival atmosphere.
setting.tone_notes: Vibrant and theatrical, mixing medieval pageantry with underlying tension. The atmosphere should feel like a dangerous dance where one wrong step could mean social death.

acts.title: Arrival and Suspicion
acts.description: William settles into the tournament grounds while navigating the complex social dynamics of noble society. A mysterious herald arrives with sealed letters, and whispers about Sir Ulrich's authenticity begin to circulate.
acts.end_condition: William discovers the contents of the herald's letters or commits to a dangerous course of action to protect his identity
locked_constraints: William's true identity must not be definitively exposed
Kate must not complete any major armor repairs

acts.title: The Lists and Lies
acts.description: The tournament begins in earnest as William competes in the lists while managing escalating threats to his identity. Political intrigue deepens as rival knights and suspicious nobles close in on the truth.
acts.end_condition: A major revelation threatens to expose William, forcing him into a desperate gambit
locked_constraints: William cannot be definitively exposed yet
The final joust has not begun
key_beats: A dangerous alliance forms against William

acts.title: Truth and Triumph
acts.description: William faces his greatest challenge as enemies close in and the final joust approaches. Everything depends on whether he can maintain his deception long enough to achieve victory or if the truth will destroy everything he's built.
acts.end_condition: William's fate is decided—his identity either secured through victory or exposed through failure

npcs.name: Roland
npcs.motivation: Protect William and see him succeed as a knight
npcs.secret: Knows William better than anyone and fears their deception will eventually be discovered

npcs.name: Wat
npcs.motivation: Serve his knight and prove his own worth
npcs.secret: Has been telling embellished stories about Sir Ulrich to other squires

npcs.name: Kate
npcs.motivation: Perfect her craft and maintain the equipment that keeps William alive
npcs.secret: Has noticed inconsistencies in William's armor that suggest it wasn't made for nobility

npcs.name: Geoffrey Chaucer
npcs.motivation: Escape his gambling debts while enjoying the thrill of deception
npcs.secret: Owes money to dangerous people who have tracked him to Rouen

npcs.name: Sir Bertrand de Montfort
npcs.motivation: Expose false knights and maintain the purity of nobility
npcs.secret: Has been investigating Sir Ulrich's background and found discrepancies

npcs.name: Lady Isabelle de Rouen
npcs.motivation: Ensure her tournament maintains its prestigious reputation
npcs.secret: Secretly admires William's skill but fears scandal if he's exposed

npcs.name: Brother Marcus
npcs.motivation: Deliver sealed letters that could change everything
npcs.secret: Carries correspondence from Gelderland that could expose William's false papers

factions.name: William's Company
factions.description: The tight-knit group of companions who support William's deception and share in his dreams of glory
factions.goals: Maintain William's noble identity and achieve tournament victory

factions.name: The Traditional Nobility
factions.description: Established knights and nobles who view newcomers with suspicion and seek to protect their exclusive social order
factions.goals: Preserve noble bloodlines and expose false claimants to knighthood

world_rules: Noble birth determines social standing and tournament eligibility
Forged papers of nobility are extremely dangerous to possess
Tournament victory brings fame but also scrutiny
Gambling debts can follow a man across kingdoms
A knight's reputation is his most valuable possession

data.rawPreview:
{
  "passage": "The morning sun cast long shadows across the tournament grounds at Rouen as Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein's party crested the hill overlooking the sprawling encampment. Colorful pavilions dotted the field like exotic flowers, their banners snapping in the Norman breeze. William Thatcher felt his heart quicken at the sight—here lay his greatest opportunity yet to cement his reputation among the nobility of Europe.\n\n'Christ's blood, look at the size of this gathering,' Roland mutt

data.passagePreview: The morning sun cast long shadows across the tournament grounds at Rouen as Sir Ulrich von Liechtens

data.passage: The morning sun cast long shadows across the tournament grounds at Rouen as Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein's party crested the final hill. Below them sprawled a magnificent spectacle—hundreds of colorful pavilions dotted the field like exotic flowers, their banners snapping in the crisp Norman breeze. The thunder of hooves echoed from practice yards where knights tested their mettle against quintains and each other. William Thatcher pulled his destrier Bayard to a halt, his heart pounding beneath the borrowed coat of arms he wore. Three months of victories had brought him here, to the most prestigious tournament in Christendom, where the greatest knights of Europe gathered to prove their worth. 'Magnificent, isn't it?' Chaucer declared, already composing flowery phrases in his head. 'The very cream of nobility, all gathered to witness the prowess of Sir Ulrich!' Roland shot their herald a warning look—such enthusiasm could draw unwanted attention. Kate adjusted the straps securing their equipment wagon while Wat gazed wide-eyed at the forest of lance pennants. As they descended toward the encampment, William noticed a richly dressed knight near the registration pavilion watching their approach with calculating eyes. The man's surcoat bore the arms of Montfort, and something in his steady gaze made William's carefully forged papers feel suddenly heavy in his saddlebags.

A.text: Ride directly to the knight watching you and introduce yourself boldly as Sir Ulrich
A.consequence: Bold introduction draws Montfort's full attention and scrutiny, potentially triggering his investigation
A.narration_directive: Show William's confident approach but hint at Montfort's growing suspicion and probing questions about Gelderland

B.text: Dismount at a nearby pavilion and send Chaucer to make discreet inquiries about the observer
B.consequence: Chaucer gathers useful information about Montfort while maintaining distance
B.narration_directive: Chaucer learns about Montfort's reputation for investigating knights' credentials, providing valuable intelligence

C.text: Guide your party toward the registration area while keeping a careful eye on the watching knight
C.consequence: Cautious approach allows observation of Montfort while proceeding with registration
C.narration_directive: William maintains composure while registering, gathering information about the tournament structure and other competitors

D.text: Suggest making camp away from the main gathering to avoid immediate scrutiny
D.consequence: Avoiding the main camp appears suspicious and delays registration, drawing more attention
D.narration_directive: The delay in registration raises questions among other knights and tournament officials about Sir Ulrich's reluctance to mingle

- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
The morning sun cast long shadows across the tournament grounds at Rouen as Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein's party crested the final hill. Below them sprawled a magnificent spectacle—hundreds of colorful pavilions dotted the field like exotic flowers, their banners snapping in the crisp Norman breeze. The thunder of hooves echoed from practice yards where knights tested their mettle against quintains and each other. William Thatcher pulled his destrier Bayard to a halt, his heart pounding beneath the borrowed coat of arms he wore. Three months of victories had brought him here, to the most prestigious tournament in Christendom, where the greatest knights of Europe gathered to prove their worth. 'Magnificent, isn't it?' Chaucer declared, already composing flowery phrases in his head. 'The very cream of nobility, all gathered to witness the prowess of Sir Ulrich!' Roland shot their herald a warning look—such enthusiasm could draw unwanted attention. Kate adjusted the straps securing their equipment wagon while Wat gazed wide-eyed at the forest of lance pennants. As they descended toward the encampment, William noticed a richly dressed knight near the registration pavilion watching their approach with calculating eyes. The man's surcoat bore the arms of Montfort, and something in his steady gaze made William's carefully forged papers feel suddenly heavy in his saddlebags.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Ride directly to the knight watching you and introduce yourself boldly as Sir Ulrich
B: Dismount at a nearby pavilion and send Chaucer to make discreet inquiries about the observer
C: Guide your party toward the registration area while keeping a careful eye on the watching knight
D: Suggest making camp away from the main gathering to avoid immediate scrutiny

data.passagePreview: William swung down from Bayard's saddle with practiced ease, his armor clanking softly as his boots

data.passage:
William swung down from Bayard's saddle with practiced ease, his armor clanking softly as his boots touched the packed earth. The morning sun caught the polished steel of his breastplate as he surveyed the bustling tournament grounds with careful eyes. Colorful pavilions stretched in orderly rows, their banners snapping in the crisp Norman breeze.

"Geoffrey," he called quietly to his herald, who was already adjusting his worn but serviceable doublet. "That knight by the registration pavilion—the one watching us like a hawk sizing up prey. Make some inquiries, but keep it casual. A few cups of wine, some idle gossip about the tournament favorites."

Chaucer's eyes gleamed with the prospect of intrigue. "Ah, reconnaissance wrapped in revelry—my specialty, my lord." He straightened his shoulders and adopted the bearing of a man comfortable in noble company. "I shall return with tales to tell."

As Chaucer sauntered toward a cluster of heralds and minor nobles near the wine merchant's stall, Roland stepped closer to William's side. "Think he suspects something?" the loyal companion murmured, his hand resting casually on his sword hilt.

Wat busied himself with Bayard's tack, but his young face was tense with worry. Kate, meanwhile, examined William's armor with her practiced eye, checking for any telltale signs that might betray their humble origins to those who knew what to look for.

A.text: Use the time to inspect the other knights' equipment and heraldry for potential threats
A.consequence: William gains valuable tactical knowledge about rival knights without drawing attention
A.narration_directive: William observes the competition and notices details about their equipment, heraldry, and potential weaknesses while remaining inconspicuous

B.text: Approach Lady Isabelle's pavilion to pay respects and gauge her disposition
B.consequence: Meeting Lady Isabelle could gain her favor but risks scrutiny of William's credentials
B.narration_directive: Lady Isabelle receives William cordially but her sharp questions about Gelderland test his knowledge and composure

C.text: Set up your own pavilion quickly to establish a proper noble presence
C.consequence: Establishing a proper pavilion improves William's noble appearance and standing
C.narration_directive: The party efficiently sets up an impressive pavilion that enhances their credibility among the other nobles

D.text: Practice jousting forms in the training yard to demonstrate skill and confidence
D.consequence: Public practice draws unwanted attention from Montfort and other suspicious knights
D.narration_directive: William's training session attracts a crowd including Montfort, who begins asking pointed questions about William's fighting style and training

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Chaucer gathers useful information about Montfort while maintaining distance"
Narration directive: "Chaucer learns about Montfort's reputation for investigating knights' credentials, providing valuable intelligence"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
William swung down from Bayard's saddle with practiced ease, his armor clanking softly as his boots touched the packed earth. The morning sun caught the polished steel of his breastplate as he surveyed the bustling tournament grounds with careful eyes. Colorful pavilions stretched in orderly rows, their banners snapping in the crisp Norman breeze.

"Geoffrey," he called quietly to his herald, who was already adjusting his worn but serviceable doublet. "That knight by the registration pavilion—the one watching us like a hawk sizing up prey. Make some inquiries, but keep it casual. A few cups of wine, some idle gossip about the tournament favorites."

Chaucer's eyes gleamed with the prospect of intrigue. "Ah, reconnaissance wrapped in revelry—my specialty, my lord." He straightened his shoulders and adopted the bearing of a man comfortable in noble company. "I shall return with tales to tell."

As Chaucer sauntered toward a cluster of heralds and minor nobles near the wine merchant's stall, Roland stepped closer to William's side. "Think he suspects something?" the loyal companion murmured, his hand resting casually on his sword hilt.

Wat busied himself with Bayard's tack, but his young face was tense with worry. Kate, meanwhile, examined William's armor with her practiced eye, checking for any telltale signs that might betray their humble origins to those who knew what to look for.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Use the time to inspect the other knights' equipment and heraldry for potential threats
B: Approach Lady Isabelle's pavilion to pay respects and gauge her disposition
C: Set up your own pavilion quickly to establish a proper noble presence
D: Practice jousting forms in the training yard to demonstrate skill and confidence

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
William moved through the encampment with practiced nonchalance, his eyes cataloging every detail while maintaining the casual air of a knight simply enjoying the morning atmosphere. The heraldic banners told stories of old bloodlines and recent victories—the three golden lions of Sir Edmund of Wessex, the silver stag of Count Philippe de Valois, and dozens more that spoke of power and legitimacy.

His gaze lingered on Sir Bertrand de Montfort's pavilion, where the man's squires were meticulously maintaining armor that bore the unmistakable marks of countless tournaments. The steel was worn smooth at the joints, scored by lance points that had found their mark. This was no ceremonial knight—Montfort was a veteran who had earned his reputation through blood and skill.

Nearby, Roland fell into step beside him, voice low. "The Flemish contingent arrived during the night. Their leader, Sir Godwin, has been asking questions about recent additions to the tournament rolls." He nodded toward a cluster of blue and gold pavilions where heavily armored knights practiced with brutal efficiency.

"Questions about what specifically?" William asked, though he suspected he knew the answer.

"About knights from distant lands. About papers and pedigrees." Roland's expression darkened. "Chaucer's been listening at the ale tents. There's talk of a herald arriving tomorrow with correspondence from various courts."

The morning sun climbed higher, and with it, the stakes of their dangerous game.

A.text: Approach Sir Godwin directly to gauge his intentions and deflect suspicion
A.consequence: Direct confrontation with Sir Godwin could backfire if he's already suspicious, but might provide crucial information
A.narration_directive: William approaches Sir Godwin with calculated confidence, but the Flemish knight's probing questions create tension and potential exposure risk

B.text: Focus on the upcoming herald's arrival and try to intercept the correspondence
B.consequence: William focuses on the herald threat and gathers information about the incoming correspondence without immediate risk
B.narration_directive: William and his companions work to learn more about the herald's mission and potential contents of the letters through careful investigation

C.text: Challenge one of the lesser knights to a practice bout to demonstrate legitimacy
C.consequence: A practice bout could demonstrate skill but also draws attention and risks injury or exposure through combat performance
C.narration_directive: William engages in a practice bout that showcases his abilities but creates new scrutiny from observing knights

D.text: Return to your pavilion and have Chaucer forge additional supporting documents
D.consequence: Forging additional documents is extremely dangerous and could be discovered, creating immediate exposure risk
D.narration_directive: Chaucer's forgery attempt goes wrong or is discovered, creating immediate danger to William's identity and safety

  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

Narration directive: "William observes the competition and notices details about their equipment, heraldry, and potential weaknesses while remaining inconspicuous"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
William moved through the encampment with practiced nonchalance, his eyes cataloging every detail while maintaining the casual air of a knight simply enjoying the morning atmosphere. The heraldic banners told stories of old bloodlines and recent victories—the three golden lions of Sir Edmund of Wessex, the silver stag of Count Philippe de Valois, and dozens more that spoke of power and legitimacy.

His gaze lingered on Sir Bertrand de Montfort's pavilion, where the man's squires were meticulously maintaining armor that bore the unmistakable marks of countless tournaments. The steel was worn smooth at the joints, scored by lance points that had found their mark. This was no ceremonial knight—Montfort was a veteran who had earned his reputation through blood and skill.

Nearby, Roland fell into step beside him, voice low. "The Flemish contingent arrived during the night. Their leader, Sir Godwin, has been asking questions about recent additions to the tournament rolls." He nodded toward a cluster of blue and gold pavilions where heavily armored knights practiced with brutal efficiency.

"Questions about what specifically?" William asked, though he suspected he knew the answer.

"About knights from distant lands. About papers and pedigrees." Roland's expression darkened. "Chaucer's been listening at the ale tents. There's talk of a herald arriving tomorrow with correspondence from various courts."

The morning sun climbed higher, and with it, the stakes of their dangerous game.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Approach Sir Godwin directly to gauge his intentions and deflect suspicion
B: Focus on the upcoming herald's arrival and try to intercept the correspondence
C: Challenge one of the lesser knights to a practice bout to demonstrate legitimacy
D: Return to your pavilion and have Chaucer forge additional supporting documents

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
William gathered his companions closer, lowering his voice as the morning bustle of the tournament grounds provided perfect cover. "The herald concerns me more than Montfort's suspicions," he said, watching a group of squires practice nearby. "Letters can contain proof that words cannot."

Chaucer's eyes gleamed with mischief. "Ah, but letters must be delivered, and heralds are only human. They eat, they drink, they sleep—and they talk when properly encouraged." He adjusted his robes with theatrical flair. "Brother Marcus, you said? I know of him. He serves the Abbey of Saint-Denis, carries correspondence for nobles throughout Normandy."

Kate looked up from checking William's stirrup leathers. "If he's bringing letters about your identity, they'll be sealed with official wax. Breaking those seals would be noticed immediately."

"Not if we never break them," Roland interjected thoughtfully. "But we could learn where they came from, who sent them, perhaps even who they're meant for."

Wat nodded eagerly. "I could befriend his traveling party—squires always know more than their masters realize."

The sound of approaching hoofbeats drew their attention to the main road. A small party crested the hill—three riders in brown robes, one carrying a leather satchel that seemed to weigh heavily against his side. Brother Marcus had arrived earlier than expected, and William felt his carefully constructed world shift beneath him like sand.

A.text: Send Chaucer to engage Brother Marcus directly with charm and conversation
A.consequence: Chaucer's direct approach risks drawing attention to William's party and may alert Brother Marcus to their interest
A.narration_directive: Chaucer engages Brother Marcus in conversation but his theatrical nature and probing questions create suspicion about his motives

B.text: Have Wat befriend the herald's servants and squires to gather information
B.consequence: Wat successfully befriends the herald's servants and learns valuable information about the letters without raising suspicion
B.narration_directive: Wat uses his natural charm and squire connections to gather intelligence from Brother Marcus's traveling companions

C.text: Follow the herald discreetly to observe who he meets and where he goes
C.consequence: Following the herald provides useful intelligence but risks being spotted by observant nobles or the herald himself
C.narration_directive: William's party attempts to shadow Brother Marcus but must navigate the crowded tournament grounds without being detected

D.text: Create a distraction to delay the herald's meetings while you gather intelligence
D.consequence: Creating a distraction draws unwanted attention from tournament officials and may implicate William's party in suspicious activity
D.narration_directive: The distraction succeeds in delaying the herald but creates complications with tournament authorities and suspicious nobles

  },

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "William focuses on the herald threat and gathers information about the incoming correspondence without immediate risk"
Narration directive: "William and his companions work to learn more about the herald's mission and potential contents of the letters through careful investigation"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
William gathered his companions closer, lowering his voice as the morning bustle of the tournament grounds provided perfect cover. "The herald concerns me more than Montfort's suspicions," he said, watching a group of squires practice nearby. "Letters can contain proof that words cannot."

Chaucer's eyes gleamed with mischief. "Ah, but letters must be delivered, and heralds are only human. They eat, they drink, they sleep—and they talk when properly encouraged." He adjusted his robes with theatrical flair. "Brother Marcus, you said? I know of him. He serves the Abbey of Saint-Denis, carries correspondence for nobles throughout Normandy."

Kate looked up from checking William's stirrup leathers. "If he's bringing letters about your identity, they'll be sealed with official wax. Breaking those seals would be noticed immediately."

"Not if we never break them," Roland interjected thoughtfully. "But we could learn where they came from, who sent them, perhaps even who they're meant for."

Wat nodded eagerly. "I could befriend his traveling party—squires always know more than their masters realize."

The sound of approaching hoofbeats drew their attention to the main road. A small party crested the hill—three riders in brown robes, one carrying a leather satchel that seemed to weigh heavily against his side. Brother Marcus had arrived earlier than expected, and William felt his carefully constructed world shift beneath him like sand.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Send Chaucer to engage Brother Marcus directly with charm and conversation
B: Have Wat befriend the herald's servants and squires to gather information
C: Follow the herald discreetly to observe who he meets and where he goes
D: Create a distraction to delay the herald's meetings while you gather intelligence

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
Wat nodded eagerly at William's suggestion, his youthful face brightening with purpose. "Leave it to me, my lord. Squires always know the best gossip." He adjusted his belt and strode toward the herald's modest pavilion with the easy confidence of youth.

Within the hour, Wat returned with valuable intelligence gleaned from Brother Marcus's traveling companions over shared ale and complaints about the road. "The letters are from Gelderland, my lord," he reported quietly, glancing around to ensure they weren't overheard. "Three sealed documents—one bearing the seal of Count Adhemar himself. The servants whisper that Brother Marcus seemed troubled after reading his instructions in Ghent."

Roland's expression darkened at the mention of Adhemar's seal. "Nothing good ever comes from that quarter," he muttered.

Chaucer stroked his chin thoughtfully. "Count Adhemar has been known to investigate... irregularities in noble lineages. If he's corresponding about Gelderland nobility..."

"Then we need to know what those letters contain before Brother Marcus delivers them," Kate said bluntly, setting down her hammer. "The question is how."

From across the camp, Brother Marcus emerged from his pavilion, the sealed letters visible in his leather satchel as he began walking purposefully toward the tournament officials' quarters.

A.text: Follow Brother Marcus discreetly to see who receives the letters
A.consequence: William learns who receives the letters but risks being spotted by Brother Marcus or tournament officials
A.narration_directive: William follows at a distance but must make difficult choices about how close to get without being detected

B.text: Have Chaucer create a distraction while someone searches the herald's pavilion
B.consequence: The search attempt goes wrong - either they're caught in the act or the distraction draws unwanted attention from Sir Bertrand
B.narration_directive: Chaucer's distraction works initially but something goes catastrophically wrong during the search

C.text: Approach Brother Marcus directly and attempt to learn his business through conversation
C.consequence: William engages Brother Marcus in diplomatic conversation and learns some information without raising immediate suspicion
C.narration_directive: William uses his noble persona to approach Brother Marcus with apparent casual interest in his journey

D.text: Send word to Lady Isabelle requesting an urgent audience about tournament matters
D.consequence: Lady Isabelle's audience reveals she knows more about the situation than expected, forcing William to confront the truth about his identity
D.narration_directive: Lady Isabelle's response to William's request reveals she has been aware of the investigation and forces a crucial decision about his identity

      ],

  },

    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses. Non-lethal conditions auto-expire when their timer runs out. Lethal conditions persist past expiry.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: { id: "hunted_by_guards_001", name: "Hunted by Guards", type: "threat", description: "The city watch is searching for you after the tavern brawl", severity: 0.6, time_remaining: { hours: 2 } }. Threats ALWAYS persist past timer expiry — they never go away on their own. When a threat's timer expires, it has TRIGGERED. You MUST resolve it next turn: remove it from the list and reflect its impact (new conditions, relationship changes, world flag changes, etc.).
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- Use threats to represent any approaching danger or ticking time-bomb: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines, etc.
- The client ticks down all status effect timers (conditions AND threats) by time_elapsed each turn.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. If an effect will kill the player, there should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

Pre-determined consequence: "Wat successfully befriends the herald's servants and learns valuable information about the letters without raising suspicion"
Narration directive: "Wat uses his natural charm and squire connections to gather intelligence from Brother Marcus's traveling companions"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

PASSAGE:
Wat nodded eagerly at William's suggestion, his youthful face brightening with purpose. "Leave it to me, my lord. Squires always know the best gossip." He adjusted his belt and strode toward the herald's modest pavilion with the easy confidence of youth.

Within the hour, Wat returned with valuable intelligence gleaned from Brother Marcus's traveling companions over shared ale and complaints about the road. "The letters are from Gelderland, my lord," he reported quietly, glancing around to ensure they weren't overheard. "Three sealed documents—one bearing the seal of Count Adhemar himself. The servants whisper that Brother Marcus seemed troubled after reading his instructions in Ghent."

Roland's expression darkened at the mention of Adhemar's seal. "Nothing good ever comes from that quarter," he muttered.

Chaucer stroked his chin thoughtfully. "Count Adhemar has been known to investigate... irregularities in noble lineages. If he's corresponding about Gelderland nobility..."

"Then we need to know what those letters contain before Brother Marcus delivers them," Kate said bluntly, setting down her hammer. "The question is how."

From across the camp, Brother Marcus emerged from his pavilion, the sealed letters visible in his leather satchel as he began walking purposefully toward the tournament officials' quarters.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Follow Brother Marcus discreetly to see who receives the letters
B: Have Chaucer create a distraction while someone searches the herald's pavilion
C: Approach Brother Marcus directly and attempt to learn his business through conversation
D: Send word to Lady Isabelle requesting an urgent audience about tournament matters

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:

data.passage:
William straightened his shoulders and adopted the bearing expected of Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein. With measured steps, he approached Brother Marcus near the officials' pavilion, his companions trailing at a respectful distance. The herald looked up from adjusting his travel satchel, his weathered face showing polite curiosity.

"Brother Marcus, I presume?" William offered a courteous nod. "I am Sir Ulrich von Liechtenstein of Gelderland. Word travels quickly in these grounds—I heard you've journeyed far to reach us."

The herald's eyes brightened with recognition. "Ah, Sir Ulrich! Your reputation precedes you, good knight. Indeed, I've ridden hard from the Low Countries with correspondence for Lady Isabelle and the tournament officials." He patted his satchel meaningfully. "Official matters regarding participant credentials and... other concerns."

William's pulse quickened, but he maintained his noble composure. "Gelderland business, perhaps? I confess some curiosity—it has been months since I've had news from my homeland."

Brother Marcus studied him with shrewd eyes. "Some correspondence does bear Count Adhemar's seal, though much of it concerns administrative verification processes. The Count has been... particular about ensuring proper documentation lately." His tone carried subtle weight. "Fascinating how thoroughly he investigates matters of noble lineage these days."

Behind William, he could sense Chaucer's tension like a coiled spring. The herald was clearly fishing, but hadn't yet revealed his full hand.

A.text: Press for more details about Count Adhemar's investigations while maintaining friendly interest
A.consequence: William gains more specific information about the investigation but raises Brother Marcus's suspicions about his interest
A.narration_directive: Brother Marcus becomes more guarded and starts asking pointed questions about William's background

B.text: Offer to escort Brother Marcus to the officials, hoping to learn more during the walk
B.consequence: William accompanies Brother Marcus and learns additional details while maintaining his cover
B.narration_directive: The walk provides natural conversation opportunities and William gathers intelligence without seeming overly interested

C.text: Deflect the conversation toward tournament gossip and withdraw gracefully
C.consequence: William gracefully exits the conversation without raising suspicion but gains no additional information
C.narration_directive: William smoothly transitions to tournament talk and withdraws, leaving Brother Marcus with a positive impression but no new intelligence gained

D.text: Signal Chaucer to create a distraction while you attempt to glimpse the letters
D.consequence: The distraction attempt fails and Brother Marcus becomes highly suspicious of William's motives, potentially alerting officials
D.narration_directive: Chaucer's distraction is clumsy and obvious, making Brother Marcus realize William was trying to spy on the correspondence
